topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights hit the canvas with an almost aggressive brilliance, illuminating swirls of ochre and deep indigo that were unmistakably mine. I stood near the entrance, my breath catching in my throat, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. People milled around the piece, murmuring appreciatively, their gazes lingering on the texture, the brushstrokes I had poured my very soul into. I took a tentative step forward, my shoe scuffing the polished floor, and tried to find a familiar face, a curator, anyone who could explain this sudden, bewildering invasion of my space. Every smile I received felt brittle, like thin ice about to crack, and I kept glancing back at the painting, a silent plea etched into my posture: *Where is this coming from?*

[story 2]

It was an afternoon of quiet contemplation, the scent of turpentine heavy in the air of my studio, when I noticed it. Hung prominently on the main wall, bathed in the cool, sterile glow of the exhibition hall, was my latest series. My heart gave a sudden, uneven thump against my ribs. I walked closer, my hands instinctively reaching out as if to touch the surface, but I stopped short, hovering just inches away. A woman with impeccably styled hair was leaning in, pointing, her expression one of intense, proprietary interest. "Isn't this stunning?" she breathed. The sound of her voice felt alien in the echoing space. I felt a strange detachment, as if I were watching a performance I hadn't auditioned for, my jaw tightening as I struggled to formulate a response that didn't betray the unsettling question churning inside me.

[story 3]

I found myself standing in the corner of the viewing room, watching the audience interact with my work. The way they leaned in, the hushed reverence they showed—it was all so… external. My own creation, the very language I spoke on the canvas, was being interpreted by strangers as if it were a relic unearthed from some forgotten tomb. A flush crept up my neck, warming my skin. I watched a group of critics circle the piece, their low conversations punctuated by nods of approval. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, a restless energy buzzing beneath my skin. I wanted to step forward, to demand an explanation, but my feet seemed rooted to the spot, paralyzed by the sheer, unexpected strangeness of the situation.
